summary: Deep research: rigorous text-based knowledge-graph architecture for stock-market information (KR+US), heavy-ingest/1-2-consumer, AI structures info & humans judge

evaluations.name: Microsoft GraphRAG (original / global+local search)
evaluations.whatItIs: Microsoft Research's reference pipeline: LLM extracts entities/relations from a corpus, builds a graph, runs Leiden community detection, and LLM-summarizes each community into reports for 'global' query answering. Outputs hierarchical communities + summaries.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Its community-clustering + hierarchical summarization is exactly the 'clusters/hierarchy/importance' structure the GOAL wants, and it is descriptive (no buy/sell signal), so it respects the human-judges constraint. BUT it is fundamentally BATCH/STATIC: the graph is built once from a fixed corpus and keeping it fresh requires periodic full re-index, which is expensive in LLM cost. That collides head-on with HEAVY CONTINUOUS INGEST and the LLM-cost-tiering constraint. A study cited ~16.6% accuracy drop on time-sensitive queries vs plain RAG. Provides nothing for KR+EN entity resolution to a ticker, nothing bi-temporal, nothing for provenance weighting.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Assumes a static corpus. On a continuously-updating news/community feed you either pay full-rebuild LLM cost repeatedly or run a stale graph. LazyGraphRAG defers some cost but does not make ingestion incremental. No point-in-time/bitemporal support → lookahead-bias risk for a human drawing conclusions.
evaluations.cost: High recurring LLM cost (full community summarization on rebuild). Compute modest at 1-2 users. Operationally a Python batch pipeline.

evaluations.name: LightRAG
evaluations.whatItIs: Lightweight graph+vector RAG (HKUDS, EMNLP 2025). Dual-level retrieval over an LLM-extracted entity/relation graph plus embeddings. Explicitly designed to lower the cost of incremental updates.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Best incremental story of the GraphRAG family: new documents go through local graph indexing and are merged into the existing graph via set-union, no global re-index — roughly halving update cost vs Microsoft GraphRAG. That directly serves heavy continuous ingest. Pluggable stores (works with pgvector/Neo4j) fit a single-node deployment for 1-2 users. Still descriptive structure, not a trading signal. Gaps: no bi-temporal model, no ticker-anchored KR+EN entity resolution, no provenance/credibility weighting — all left to you.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Incremental merge is additive — it grows the graph but has weak built-in handling of fact supersession/correction over time (no validity intervals), so temporal correctness must be added on top. Entity dedup is LLM/string based, not anchored to DART/CIK/ticker. Treat as a retrieval layer, not the temporal source of truth.
evaluations.cost: Per-document extraction LLM cost (mitigate with the cost-tiering pre-filters). Low compute/ops at this scale; single Python process + chosen store.

evaluations.name: nano-graphrag
evaluations.whatItIs: Minimal (~1k LOC) hackable reimplementation of GraphRAG's core; the codebase LightRAG itself was built on. Pluggable backends (Faiss, Neo4j, Ollama, etc.).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Value here is as a transparent, modifiable base when rigor demands you control every step (entity-resolution keying, provenance fields, cost gates) rather than fight a heavy framework. At 1-2 users, small-and-hackable is an asset. But it inherits GraphRAG's batch orientation — incremental update is not a first-class guarantee; you would be building it. Same gaps on temporal/entity/provenance as the family.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: It's scaffolding, not a product: you own correctness. Good if you intend to hand-build the custom layers anyway; wasteful if you expected batteries included. Maintenance/velocity lower than LightRAG.
evaluations.cost: Low. It's a library; cost is your engineering time plus the same per-item extraction LLM spend.

evaluations.name: HippoRAG (2/neurobiological)
evaluations.whatItIs: Retrieval method using a Personalized-PageRank pass over an LLM-built KG (a 'neurobiologically inspired' single-step multi-hop), to surface the most relevant nodes for a query quickly.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Its PPR retrieval is genuinely useful and conceptually adjacent to what you need for IMPORTANCE ranking — but note PPR ranks by graph topology/relevance, which is precisely the 'LOUD over TRUE' failure the credibility constraint warns about unless you inject source-trust priors into the PPR personalization vector. So it is a building block for the custom ranking layer, not a turnkey answer. It is a retrieval algorithm, not a continuously-updating store; incremental ingestion is not its concern.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Pure PPR/centrality without trust priors amplifies adversarial pump/rumor sources (종토방/Reddit/Telegram). Usable only if personalized with provenance weights. Not a temporal or storage solution.
evaluations.cost: Cheap at query time (single PPR pass). Index build still incurs LLM extraction cost.

evaluations.name: RAPTOR (recursive clustering + summarization tree)
evaluations.whatItIs: Builds a tree by recursively embedding, clustering, and LLM-summarizing chunks bottom-up, giving multi-resolution summaries for retrieval at varying abstraction levels.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Directly produces the CLUSTERS + HIERARCHY the GOAL asks for, and as multi-level thematic summaries it's a strong human-navigation aid (e.g. 'what is the market saying about KOSDAQ biotech this week'). But the tree is built upfront and is hostile to incremental updates — new documents force rebuild/major modification. So it cannot be the live store under continuous ingest. The right role is a PERIODIC OFFLINE ENRICHMENT pass (e.g. nightly) that re-clusters a time-windowed slice into navigable theme trees on top of the live graph+vector store.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Treating RAPTOR as the primary index under streaming data leads to constant expensive rebuilds. Bounded by running it offline over fixed time windows. No entity resolution, no provenance, no bitemporal — purely a summarization/clustering layer.
evaluations.cost: High LLM cost per rebuild (summarize every cluster every level) → run on a schedule over windowed data, not continuously.

evaluations.name: Graphiti (Zep) — temporal KG core
evaluations.whatItIs: Open-source (20k+ stars) framework for REAL-TIME, incrementally-updated temporal knowledge graphs. Ingests 'episodes', extracts and immediately resolves entities/edges against the existing graph, and is genuinely BI-TEMPORAL: every edge carries valid-time (t_valid/t_invalid, when true in the world) and ingestion-time (when observed). Superseded facts are invalidated, not deleted. Custom entity types via Pydantic. Now uses deterministic IR front-ends + entropy-gated fuzzy matching for dedup, falling back to LLM only when needed.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The single best fit for the two hardest constraints. Bi-temporal modeling (constraint 2) is built in and exactly answers event-time vs ingest-time / no-lookahead-bias for a human. Incremental-by-design (constraint 4 friendly, constraint 5 friendly) suits heavy continuous ingest at 1-2 users far better than batch GraphRAG. Custom Pydantic entities + IR/fuzzy dedup give a real head start on KR+EN resolution (constraint 1) — though you must still anchor canonical identity to DART corp code / SEC CIK / ticker yourself; it won't know 삼성전자=Samsung=005930 without alias seeding. Descriptive memory layer, no trading signal → respects GOAL. Does NOT do source-credibility weighting — that stays custom.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Requires a graph backend: Neo4j 5.26+ or FalkorDB (Kuzu is deprecated; Neptune is cloud) — so it is NOT a Postgres-only solution; this is the main cost of adopting it. Per-episode extraction is LLM-heavy → the cost-tiering pre-filters are mandatory upstream. Entity resolution quality on Korean depends on you seeding aliases + ticker keys. Provenance weighting must be added.
evaluations.cost: Runs single-node on FalkorDB (lightweight, Redis-protocol, Dockerable; embedded FalkorDB-Lite emerging) — cheap to operate for 2 users. Dominant cost is LLM extraction per episode, controlled by pre-filtering.

evaluations.name: Neo4j native vector index (graph + vectors in one engine)
evaluations.whatItIs: Neo4j's built-in HNSW vector index (since 5.11; up to 4096 dims, cosine/euclidean) so embeddings live beside graph nodes, queried with Cypher alongside traversals.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Co-locating vectors with the graph removes a moving part and lets one Cypher query mix semantic search with multi-hop traversal — attractive for a single-node, 1-2-user system where operational simplicity beats raw vector latency. Vector search is slower than a dedicated engine (~30-100ms vs single-digit ms), but at two consumers that is irrelevant. It is the natural vector layer IF Neo4j is already your graph backend (e.g. via Graphiti on Neo4j).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Only justified when Neo4j is already in the stack; adopting Neo4j solely for vectors is wrong. Community edition single-node has no clustering, but you don't need clustering here. Bilingual embeddings still require a good multilingual embed model — the index is language-agnostic.
evaluations.cost: Free community edition runs single-node fine; one process to operate. Latency higher than Qdrant but a non-issue at this load.

evaluations.name: Dedicated vector store (Qdrant / Weaviate / Milvus)
evaluations.whatItIs: Standalone HNSW vector databases with rich payload filtering and sub-10ms latency (Qdrant ~4ms p50 on 1M 768-d vectors; strong filtered-search performance).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Their advantage is speed and scale under heavy query/serving load — which this system does NOT have (1-2 consumers, tiny serving). They add a separate stateful service to snapshot, secure (gRPC), monitor, and version against your primary DB. That operational overhead is hard to justify against constraint 5 (don't over-engineer serving). Speed wins are real but irrelevant here.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: You'd be paying ops complexity for latency/scale you'll never use. Only sensible if you outgrow pgvector/Neo4j vectors (tens of millions of vectors) — unlikely soon at this consumer count. Reconsider only if embedding volume from heavy ingest balloons past single-node pgvector comfort (~50M).
evaluations.cost: Extra service to run/back up/secure; self-managed minimum ~2 nodes (~$220/mo) vs free in-DB options. Compute fine; ops cost is the real tax.

evaluations.name: Postgres + pgvector + Apache AGE (single-node 'everything store')
evaluations.whatItIs: One PostgreSQL instance: pgvector for embeddings/semantic search, Apache AGE for openCypher graph queries, plus normal relational tables for source metadata, alias/ticker tables, provenance, and bi-temporal validity columns. Both extensions share the planner so a CTE can chain pgvector ANN → AGE Cypher in one transaction.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Operationally the simplest possible answer to constraint 5: ONE engine, one backup, one transaction boundary covering relational + graph + vector. Ideal home for the custom layers the off-the-shelf tools don't provide — ticker-anchored entity resolution tables (DART/KIND/CIK + KR/EN aliases), bi-temporal columns (event_time, ingest_time, valid_from/valid_to), provenance & source-credibility scores, and the LLM-cost-tiering bookkeeping (dedup hashes, embedding-similarity, salience). The cost is that you BUILD bi-temporal semantics and entity resolution by hand rather than getting Graphiti's for free.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Apache AGE is actively maintained (PG 11-18) but operationally fiddly — no reliable prebuilt image; install from source; lags on newest PG majors. AGE Cypher is less mature/performant than Neo4j for deep traversals (fine at this scale). Biggest risk: hand-rolled bi-temporal + entity resolution is exactly where rigor can slip — you must implement validity-interval supersession and ticker-keyed identity carefully or you reintroduce lookahead bias and node-splitting noise.
evaluations.cost: Lowest ops cost — single Postgres you already know; free. Cost shifts to engineering time to implement temporal + resolution logic correctly.

evaluations.name: Obsidian (human presentation / markdown layer)
evaluations.whatItIs: Local markdown notes app with bidirectional links and a graph view. Files are plain .md on disk.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Correctly placed as the PRESENTATION layer on top, never the ingestion backend. For 1-2 humans making the judgment, exporting the ranked/clustered graph as linked markdown (entity pages with provenance-weighted importance, temporal context, source citations, cluster/theme notes from the RAPTOR pass) gives a navigable, auditable surface — directly serving the GOAL (human navigates and decides). Bilingual KR/EN markdown is trivial.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Fails if abused as the store — its graph view is cosmetic, no real query engine, no temporal logic, poor at scale. Must be a generated, read-mostly export from the DB (one-way, regenerated), not a source of truth. Risk of drift if humans edit notes that the pipeline overwrites — make it clearly derived/read-only or namespace human annotations.
evaluations.cost: Free, local, zero infra. Cost is writing the DB→markdown exporter.

landscape.recommendation:
Build a LAYERED single-node stack, and resolve the one spine decision — BUY the hard temporal/entity core, BUILD the provenance and resolution layers — as follows.

SPINE: The two constraints hardest to get right by hand are bi-temporal correctness (no lookahead bias) and entity resolution. Bi-temporal is the one to BUY: adopt Graphiti as the temporal-KG core because it ships genuine bi-temporal modeling (valid-time + ingest-time, fact supersession via validity intervals) and is incremental-by-design — the right shape for heavy continuous ingest at 1-2 users, and the opposite of batch Microsoft GraphRAG. Run Graphiti on FalkorDB (lightweight, single-node, Dockerable/embeddable) rather than Neo4j to keep ops minimal; Kuzu is deprecated so don't use it.

LAYERS (ingest → structure → present):
1. INGEST + COST-TIERING (constraint 4, custom): crawl KR (DART, KIND, 종토방/네이버/토스) + US (SEC EDGAR, Reddit/X, crypto TG/Discord) → cheap pre-filters BEFORE any expensive LLM call: exact/near-dup hashing, embedding-similarity drop, and a salience/credibility gate. Only survivors hit LLM extraction. This is mandatory because every recommended structuring tool is LLM-per-item.
2. CANONICAL ENTITY RESOLUTION (constraint 1, custom — your #1 build): a relational table in Postgres keying every entity to an AUTHORITATIVE ID (DART corp code, KIND, SEC CIK, ticker e.g. 005930) with KR/EN/ticker alias tables. LLM/embedding similarity is fallback only. Feed these as Graphiti custom (Pydantic) entity types + seeded aliases so 삼성전자 / Samsung Electronics / 005930 collapse to one node. Without this the graph is noise.
3. TEMPORAL KG CORE (constraint 2, BUY): Graphiti on FalkorDB holds entities/edges with bi-temporal validity → a human can query 'as known on date D' with no lookahead.
4. SEMANTIC RETRIEVAL: vectors via FalkorDB's vector support or a pgvector table (either fine at this scale; do NOT stand up Qdrant/Weaviate/Milvus — their speed/scale buys nothing for 2 consumers and adds a stateful service to babysit). Use a strong MULTILINGUAL embedding model for KR+EN.
5. PROVENANCE-WEIGHTED IMPORTANCE (constraint 3, custom — no off-the-shelf tool does this): do NOT rank by raw centrality/PageRank — that ranks LOUD (pump/rumor) over TRUE. Compute importance as trust-personalized PPR/weighted-centrality where the personalization vector folds source-class priors (DART/SEC filings = high; mainstream news = mid; 종토방/Reddit/Telegram = low/adversarial) times recency and corroboration count. Research backs the technique (GENI, CrediRAG, probabilistic-KG confidence scores) but you implement it. Importance stays a transparent, explainable score — never an AI buy/sell call (GOAL).
6. PERIODIC ENRICHMENT (offline): run RAPTOR-style recursive clustering/summarization over time-windowed slices on a schedule to produce the navigable CLUSTER/HIERARCHY/THEME view — as an offline pass, never the live store.
7. PRESENTATION: export the ranked/clustered/temporal graph to Obsidian as generated, read-mostly bilingual markdown (entity pages with provenance-weighted importance, validity windows, source citations, theme notes) so the 1-2 humans navigate and decide.

If you cannot stomach two storage engines (FalkorDB + Postgres), the fallback is the all-Postgres variant: Postgres + pgvector + Apache AGE as the single everything-store, implementing bi-temporal columns and resolution yourself. It is operationally simplest but moves the hardest correctness work onto you — acceptable only because rigor is required and time is not constrained. My lead recommendation keeps Postgres for the custom layers (resolution, provenance, cost-tiering bookkeeping) and Graphiti/FalkorDB for the temporal graph, because hand-rolling correct bi-temporal supersession is the single easiest place for rigor to silently fail.

Rule OUT explicitly: Microsoft GraphRAG (batch/static — wrong for continuous ingest); standalone Qdrant/Weaviate/Milvus (over-engineered serving for 2 users); Kafka/Spark/distributed Neo4j (scale you don't have); Obsidian-as-backend (presentation only).
risks: Entity resolution is the make-or-break and the largest custom build: cross-lingual identity (삼성전자=Samsung=005930) will NOT resolve from LLM/string dedup alone. If you don't anchor canonical nodes to authoritative IDs (DART corp code, KIND, SEC CIK, ticker) with seeded KR/EN alias tables, nodes split and the entire graph degrades to noise — every downstream layer inherits the error.

Provenance-weighted importance has NO off-the-shelf implementation. If you fall back to raw centrality/PageRank/PPR (as HippoRAG/standard KG tools do), adversarial sources (종토방, Reddit, crypto Telegram pumps) rank as 'important' — LOUD beats TRUE, directly violating constraint 3. You must build trust-personalized ranking and keep it explainable so the human, not the AI, judges.

Bi-temporal correctness is easy to get subtly wrong. If you take the all-Postgres path and hand-roll validity intervals, any leak of ingest-time into event-time reasoning reintroduces lookahead bias — a human could 'conclude' something using data that didn't exist at the decision date. Buying Graphiti's bitemporal model mitigates this; the fallback does not.

LLM extraction cost scales with HEAVY ingest. Every recommended structuring tool (Graphiti, LightRAG, RAPTOR) is LLM-per-item. If the cheap pre-filter tier (dedup → embedding-similarity → salience/credibility gate) is weak or absent, cost explodes regardless of which store you pick. The pre-filter is not optional.

Two-engine stack (FalkorDB + Postgres) adds a second backup/restore and consistency boundary. Mitigated by tiny scale, but if cross-engine consistency (graph node vs its Postgres resolution/provenance row) drifts, importance and identity desync. The all-Postgres fallback removes this risk at the cost of building bitemporal/resolution by hand.

Apache AGE (if chosen) is operationally fiddly: no reliable prebuilt image, source install, and it trails the newest Postgres majors; its Cypher is less mature than Neo4j/FalkorDB for deep traversals (acceptable at this scale, but a setup-time and upgrade-time risk).

GraphRAG/RAPTOR clustering quality on bilingual, noisy community text is unproven for Korean retail-investor slang; theme clusters may be incoherent without a strong multilingual embedding model and KR-aware extraction prompts. Validate clusters on real 종토방/Reddit samples before trusting the hierarchy.

Obsidian presentation drift: if humans edit generated notes that the pipeline later overwrites, annotations are lost. Keep the export one-way/read-mostly and namespace any human notes separately from generated content.
landscape.summary: At 1-2 consumers with heavy continuous ingest, the deciding axes are NOT vector-search latency or throughput (the benchmarks I gathered — Qdrant 4ms vs pgvector 11ms, 50M-vector ceilings — are near-irrelevant at this serving load and I deprioritize them deliberately). The real discriminators, in order, are: (1) genuine INCREMENTAL-update support for streaming data, (2) what each layer gives FREE vs what you must BUILD for the five make-or-break constraints, and (3) single-node operational simplicity. Through that lens, most of the GraphRAG family is batch/static and misfits continuous ingest: Microsoft GraphRAG rebuilds a static corpus (avoid), RAPTOR builds a static tree (useful only as an offline clustering pass), while LightRAG and especially Graphiti are incremental-by-design. Crucially, two of the five constraints — KR+EN entity resolution anchored to a real ticker (삼성전자=Samsung=005930), and provenance-weighted importance so TRUE beats LOUD — have NO turnkey product and must be custom architectural layers; they are also the top two risks. This is therefore a layered stack decision (graph + vector + presentation + custom resolution/provenance/cost-tiering), not a single-tool pick, and every verdict below ties back to heavy-ingest / 1-2-consumer / KR+EN / human-judges / incremental-update.

For a heavy-but-continuous ingest with only 1-2 consumers, the ingest layer should be a single-node, asset-aware batch+incremental pipeline that (a) tags provenance/credibility AT CRAWL TIME (constraint #3 — trust cannot be reconstructed retroactively), and (b) runs cheap pre-filters (dedup, language-detect, salience) BEFORE any LLM extraction (constraint #4). Source reality in 2026 splits sharply into clean structured APIs (DART, SEC EDGAR, GDELT, CCXT) versus adversarial/hostile community sources (Reddit pre-approval + AI-training ban, X pay-per-use, Telegram flood-bans). The structured-API spine is cheap and reliable; the community tier is where TOS/anti-bot risk and rumor noise concentrate, so it must be ingested cautiously and down-weighted by source credibility, never treated as ground truth. Distributed infra (Kafka/Spark/clustered graph) is unjustified at this serving scale and is ruled out. Verdicts below are bound to the KR+EN, point-in-time, provenance-weighted, LLM-cost-tiered, single-node constraints — not a generic API catalog.

evaluations.name: DART / OpenDART (KR primary-source disclosures + XBRL financials)
evaluations.whatItIs: Korea FSS official open API: original filings (XML), structured items, and XBRL financial statements for KOSPI/KOSDAQ + major unlisted firms. Free with an issued auth key.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The authoritative, low-noise spine for the KR market — the opposite of adversarial. Primary-source filings carry maximal source credibility (constraint #3) and are timestamped, which anchors point-in-time correctness (constraint #2: use the filing/disclosure event-time, not your ingest-time). Entity resolution (constraint #1) should be seeded from DART's corp_code registry (corp_code <-> 종목코드 005930 <-> name), giving a canonical KR identity table to collapse 삼성전자/Samsung/005930.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Per-key daily call quota exists and is FSS-adjustable; I could NOT confirm a specific number from the official intro/terms pages (they only state limits are posted on the homepage) — size your crawl budget conservatively and cache corp_code locally. Filings are KR-language; financial-statement XBRL is structured but narrative sections need parsing+translation. Restatements/amendments must be modeled bi-temporally or you get lookahead bias.
evaluations.cost: Free API. Cost is engineering: XBRL parsing, KR->EN normalization, and bi-temporal storage of amendments.

evaluations.name: SEC EDGAR (US/global primary-source filings + financial-statement API)
evaluations.whatItIs: data.sec.gov RESTful JSON APIs (submissions history, XBRL company-facts/company-concept) with no auth/key, plus full-text search over filings since 2001 (body + exhibits).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: US analogue of DART and equally the high-credibility spine. CIK is the canonical US entity key for constraint #1 (CIK <-> ticker <-> name); company-facts gives clean point-in-time XBRL for constraint #2. Full-text search is the cheap discovery layer that finds which filings to extract before spending LLM tokens (constraint #4).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Hard 10 requests/second fair-access cap across ALL your machines, with a declared User-Agent required; exceeding it gets you rate-limited/blocked as a 'botnet'. Throttle centrally in one worker. Ticker<->CIK mapping is many-to-many over time (tickers get reused) — resolve as-of-date, not latest.
evaluations.cost: Free, no key. Only cost is respecting 10 req/s (trivial for a 1-2 person op) and XBRL/HTML parsing.

evaluations.name: GDELT (global news graph: GKG entities/themes/tone via BigQuery)
evaluations.whatItIs: Monitors global broadcast/print/web news in ~100 languages, updates every 15 min, free. GKG 2.0 already extracts persons/orgs/companies/locations/themes/tone (CAMEO events + mentions) — a pre-built knowledge graph layer.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Specifically the right backbone for the GLOBAL news-graph requirement and a major LLM-cost saver: GDELT has ALREADY done entity/theme/tone extraction, so you ingest structure for free instead of LLM-extracting every article (constraint #4). It is also a natural relation/cluster source for the graph layer. Crucially it covers KR+global news at once. Treat tone as a feature, NOT a trading signal (respects the no-subjective-signal goal).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: GDELT indexes article metadata/URLs and its own NLP, not always full clean text — you still fetch+parse the source article for deep extraction. CAMEO/tone is machine-coded and noisy; its entity resolution is imperfect and must be reconciled into your own canonical entity table, not trusted blindly. BigQuery egress can cost if queried carelessly — filter by date/source partitions.
evaluations.cost: Dataset free; BigQuery query/egress is pay-per-scan (keep queries partition-pruned and incremental — cheap for a small op).

evaluations.name: Crawl4AI (self-hosted) vs Firecrawl (SaaS/AGPL) — web + news crawling
evaluations.whatItIs: Crawl4AI: Apache-2.0 Python lib bundling Playwright/Chromium, self-host only (cloud in closed beta), outputs LLM-ready markdown. Firecrawl: AGPL-3.0, self-hostable but primarily a managed API from ~$16/mo returning clean markdown/JSON, strong JS rendering + anti-bot.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: This is the general-web/news crawler for sources without clean APIs (네이버금융 pages, blogs, IR sites). For a 1-2 person op, Crawl4AI's permissive Apache-2.0 license and self-host keep data in-house and cost near-zero, fitting the single-node constraint; Firecrawl wins when you don't want to babysit Playwright/proxies against aggressive anti-bot sites. Recommend Crawl4AI as default with Firecrawl as a paid fallback for the few hostile JS/anti-bot targets — don't pay SaaS per-page for the easy 90%.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Crawl4AI's true cost is hidden: Playwright browser servers, proxy rotation, and DevOps time — real for one operator. Firecrawl's AGPL-3.0 self-host has copyleth implications if you ever redistribute. Both still hit site TOS and anti-bot walls; neither legalizes scraping a site that forbids it. Korean portals change HTML often — parsers will break and need monitoring.
evaluations.cost: Crawl4AI: free software + your server/proxy/LLM-token cost. Firecrawl: ~$16/mo entry SaaS scaling with volume, or self-host.

evaluations.name: Korean community sources (종토방 / 네이버 / 토스) — incl. Naver Finance / pykrx
evaluations.whatItIs: Retail-investor boards and Korean finance portals. No official public API for Naver Finance; access is via HTML scraping (e.g., pykrx scrapes KRX + Naver). KRX itself offers data but much retail sentiment lives on portal boards.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Directly the KR adversarial tier the system must capture — but it is exactly where constraint #3 bites: 종토방 is pump/rumor-heavy, so these nodes must be ingested with LOW source-credibility weight folded into importance, never ranked by raw centrality (which would surface LOUD over TRUE). Useful as a sentiment/attention SIGNAL surfaced for the human, not as fact.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Scraping Naver/portals risks TOS violation and breaks whenever HTML changes (pykrx is fragile by design; data copyright stays with the provider). Korean text needs morphological segmentation (Kiwi/MeCab-ko) before dedup/embedding or entity resolution degrades. High rumor density demands aggressive dedup + credibility down-weighting and bi-temporal capture (when SAID vs when TRUE).
evaluations.cost: No license fee; cost is fragile-scraper maintenance + proxy + legal/TOS risk + heavy normalization/credibility tooling.

evaluations.name: Reddit (US retail communities via official API / PRAW)
evaluations.whatItIs: Subreddit posts/comments via OAuth API. As of Nov 2025, Reddit requires explicit PRE-APPROVAL for ALL API access (even hobby projects; 2-4 wk review), free tier ~100 req/min OAuth, commercial use needs a contract (~$0.24/1k calls enterprise).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The obvious US-retail sentiment source, but the 2026 TOS makes it the riskiest fit. Reddit's Responsible Builder Policy EXPLICITLY prohibits using Reddit data to train ML/AI models without express consent — and an LLM-structuring pipeline reads squarely as the prohibited use. This is a near-disqualifier for the core 'LLM-structure into a DB' design, not a rate-limit nuisance.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Pre-approval gate + AI-training prohibition means compliant use likely requires a contract and a use-case that avoids model training/derived AI features; non-compliant scraping risks bans and legal exposure. Even approved, free-tier throughput is thin for heavy ingest. If used at all, restrict to human-readable structuring (relations/clusters) under explicit approval and keep it a low-weight, clearly-provenanced minor source.
evaluations.cost: Free tier exists but pre-approval-gated; commercial/enterprise contract pricing (~$0.24/1k calls) + compliance/legal overhead. Effective cost: high (policy risk).

evaluations.name: X / Twitter (US + crypto cashtag sentiment)
evaluations.whatItIs: Official X API: Basic ~$100/mo (50k posts app-level), pay-per-use beta ($0.005/read, $0.015-0.20/post) as of late 2025; Pro $5k/mo, Enterprise $42k+/mo. Strict rate limits regardless of spend.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Real-time cashtag/crypto chatter is genuinely useful as an attention signal, but the economics are hostile to a 1-2 person op doing HEAVY ingest. At $0.005/read, broad continuous firehose-style ingestion is cost-prohibitive; the API only makes sense for narrow, targeted cashtag/account pulls. Like Reddit, value is as a low-credibility sentiment signal for the human, not as fact (respects no-trading-signal goal).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Costs scale with volume fast; rate limits cap even paid usage. Bot/pump density is extreme on crypto cashtags — must be heavily credibility-down-weighted. Third-party scraping resellers violate TOS and are legally/technically unstable. Pay-per-use is closed beta — pricing may shift.
evaluations.cost: $100/mo Basic floor; pay-per-use $0.005/read makes heavy ingest expensive. Budget-cap and scope to targeted queries only.

evaluations.name: Telegram / Discord (crypto community channels)
evaluations.whatItIs: Telegram via Telethon/MTProto: channel & group message history (~100 msgs/call) is low-risk and stable; Discord via bot API on servers you can join. Public-content scraping broadly legal in US/EU though against platform TOS.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The main crypto-community tier, and the most workable of the social sources: pulling channel/group HISTORY (not member lists) is reliable and not rate-limit-prone, fitting heavy ingest. Pairs with CCXT for a complete crypto picture (prices + chatter). Again: high rumor/pump density -> low credibility weight, bi-temporal capture, dedup before extraction.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Member-list scraping (GetParticipants) triggers FloodWaitError then PeerFloodError account bans — Telegram's #1 spam vector — so capture MESSAGES ONLY, never member harvesting. ToS prohibits automation (risk but not criminal for public data). Use a dedicated account/proxy. Discord requires server membership and bot TOS compliance; many invite-only alpha groups are off-limits.
evaluations.cost: Free (API). Cost is a dedicated account, proxy rotation, flood-wait handling, and heavy credibility down-weighting + dedup.

evaluations.name: US market data: Alpaca vs Polygon.io vs yfinance
evaluations.whatItIs: Alpaca: free 10-yr historical bars with just account registration. Polygon.io: paid tiers, category-split, deep/granular. yfinance: free unofficial Yahoo scraping.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The price/volume backbone for US tickers, feeding point-in-time correctness (constraint #2) and the entity table (ticker<->CIK). For a 1-2 person op, Alpaca's free 10-yr history (registration only) is the pragmatic default; Polygon paid only if you need tick-level depth. yfinance is fine for ad-hoc exploration but NOT a dependency for a continuous pipeline.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: yfinance is unofficial scraping — aggressive rate limits, IP bans, inconsistent data; do not build the pipeline on it. Alpaca data depth/granularity is 'bonus-feature' quality with occasional missing volume on illiquid names — acceptable for a knowledge structure, weak for HFT (irrelevant here). Adjust for splits/dividends as-of-date to avoid lookahead.
evaluations.cost: Alpaca free (account only); yfinance free but unreliable; Polygon paid (per-category subscriptions) — only if depth needed.

evaluations.name: Crypto exchange data via CCXT (Upbit, Binance, etc.)
evaluations.whatItIs: Unified Python/JS API over 100+ exchanges (incl. Upbit for KR, Binance global) with standardized fetch_ohlcv historical candles, order books, trades, and built-in per-endpoint + account rate-limit backoff/retry.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The right abstraction for the crypto price tier — one interface instead of N bespoke exchange clients, fitting a small team. Built-in rate limiting respects the single-worker/throttle design. Upbit support covers the KR crypto angle; pairs with Telegram chatter for the crypto knowledge cluster.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Per-exchange quirks leak through the 'unified' API (Binance weight-based vs simpler per-second limits); some endpoints/history depth vary by exchange. Exchange API keys + IP allowlisting needed for higher limits. Crypto data is 24/7 and high-volume — partition/incremental ingest, don't full-reload.
evaluations.cost: Library free/open-source; exchange APIs mostly free for market data within rate limits. Cost is throttling + storage of high-frequency series.

evaluations.name: Text parsing & normalization stack (trafilatura + datasketch MinHash-LSH + lingua + Kiwi/MeCab-ko)
evaluations.whatItIs: HTML->clean text via trafilatura (best mean F1 ~0.937 / precision ~0.978, with readability/jusText fallbacks); near-dup dedup via datasketch MinHash-LSH (sub-quadratic); language detection via lingua/fastText; Korean segmentation via Kiwi or MeCab-ko.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: This IS the constraint-#4 cost-tier engine and the constraint-#1 enabler. Order matters: clean-text -> language-detect -> MinHash-LSH dedup/cluster -> salience filter -> ONLY THEN expensive LLM extraction. KR segmentation (Kiwi/MeCab-ko) is mandatory before KR embedding/dedup/entity-resolution or 삼성전자 won't reliably collapse to its canonical node. This layer is what keeps the LLM bill bounded under heavy ingest.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: trafilatura is heuristic — quality drops on JS-heavy/SPA pages (pair with Crawl4AI/Firecrawl rendering) and non-article layouts (boards, forums). MinHash needs tuned shingle size + threshold or you over/under-merge near-dups (esp. reposted KR board content). KR morphological analyzers lose spacing info and can be hard to reconstruct to source text — keep raw + normalized side by side. Mixed KR+EN docs need per-segment language routing.
evaluations.cost: All open-source/free; cost is CPU for dedup/segmentation (cheap vs LLM tokens it saves) and tuning effort.

evaluations.name: Orchestration: single-node Dagster/Prefect over bare cron (Kafka/Spark ruled out)
evaluations.whatItIs: Lightweight asset-aware orchestrator (Dagster or Prefect) on ONE box, running batch + incremental ingestion with partitions, retries, backfill, and lineage. Cron+queue+single worker is the minimalist alternative.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Constraint #5 (heavy ingest, 1-2 consumers) directly favors single-node. The discriminating question isn't vendor features but operations: with many FLAKY continuous sources (anti-bot breaks, FloodWait, rate-limit 429s, HTML drift) you DO need retry visibility, backfill, and lineage to know the blast radius of a failure — which bare cron lacks. So: one orchestrator process on a single node, NOT a cluster. Dagster's asset/partition model also maps cleanly to bi-temporal incremental ingest (constraint #2).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Risk is over-engineering — a full Dagster deployment can be heavier than a solo op wants; if the pipeline is small, cron + a durable queue (e.g., SQLite/Redis-backed) + one worker is genuinely enough and should be the fallback. Kafka/Spark/distributed-Neo4j are NOT justified at 1-2 consumers and are ruled out: they add ops burden with zero serving-scale payoff.
evaluations.cost: Open-source self-host on one node; cost is the operator's setup/learning time. No distributed-infra cost.

landscape.recommendation: Build the ingest layer as a single-node, asset-aware batch+incremental pipeline (Dagster or Prefect on ONE box — not Kafka/Spark) structured as a TWO-TIER source strategy with two non-negotiable cross-cutting stages. Tier 1, the high-credibility structured spine: DART (KR) + SEC EDGAR (US) for primary filings/XBRL, GDELT for the free global news graph (it pre-extracts entities/themes/tone, saving enormous LLM cost), Alpaca + CCXT/Upbit/Binance for prices. These are cheap, reliable, and carry maximal source-credibility weight. Tier 2, the adversarial community layer (종토방/Naver via Crawl4AI self-host, Telegram channel-history via Telethon, X only for narrow cashtag pulls): ingest cautiously, MESSAGES-not-members on Telegram, and fold LOW source-credibility weight so importance never ranks LOUD over TRUE (constraint #3). AVOID building the core on Reddit — its Nov-2025 pre-approval gate plus explicit prohibition on AI/ML training make it a policy liability for an LLM-structuring pipeline; include it only under explicit approval as a minor, clearly-provenanced signal. Two stages are mandatory and belong in the INGEST layer, not the DB: (1) tag provenance + source-credibility AT CRAWL TIME and stamp event-time vs ingest-time bi-temporally (constraints #2,#3 — trust and point-in-time correctness cannot be reconstructed later); (2) run the cheap pre-filter cascade clean-text(trafilatura) -> language-detect(lingua) -> Korean-segment(Kiwi/MeCab-ko) -> MinHash-LSH dedup(datasketch) -> salience gate BEFORE any LLM extraction (constraint #4 — this is what keeps the bill bounded under heavy ingest). Seed entity resolution (constraint #1) from authoritative registries — DART corp_code and SEC CIK as canonical KR/US keys — and resolve all aliases (삼성전자/Samsung/005930) against them as-of-date, with KR morphological normalization applied first. This respects every hard constraint, stays sized to 1-2 operators, and keeps the AI structuring information rather than emitting trading signals.
risks: Could not confirm a specific OpenDART daily call quota from official intro/terms pages (they only state limits are posted on the homepage and are FSS-adjustable) — size the KR crawl budget conservatively and verify the live number before scaling.

Reddit's Nov-2025 Responsible Builder Policy explicitly bars using Reddit data to train ML/AI without express consent; an LLM-structuring DB may read as the prohibited use — treat as a legal/compliance blocker, not a rate-limit detail.

Scraping-based KR sources (Naver Finance, pykrx) and general web crawlers break whenever site HTML changes and may violate portal TOS; budget ongoing parser-maintenance and accept fragility on the community tier.

Telegram member-list scraping triggers FloodWait then PeerFlood account bans (Telegram's top spam vector) — restrict strictly to channel/group message history; a careless member pull can burn the account in days.
X pay-per-use ($0.005/read) makes broad continuous ingestion cost-prohibitive for a small op and is still closed beta — scope to targeted cashtag/account queries with a hard budget cap.

Adversarial sources (종토방/Reddit/Telegram/X cashtags) are pump/rumor-dense; if source-credibility weighting isn't enforced, pure graph centrality will surface manipulation (LOUD) over truth (TRUE) — credibility weighting must be load-bearing, not cosmetic.

GDELT and GDELT-class machine NLP (CAMEO/tone, auto entity-coding) is noisy and must be reconciled into your own canonical entity table, never trusted as ground-truth resolution; BigQuery egress can cost if queries aren't partition-pruned.

Over-engineering risk in reverse: if the pipeline stays small, a full orchestrator may exceed need — keep cron + durable queue + single worker as a viable fallback, and hold the line against any Kafka/Spark/clustered-graph creep that the 1-2-consumer serving scale cannot justify.

landscape.summary: The extraction layer's tightest constraint is the GOAL — AI structures, a human judges, so every node/edge MUST carry source-span + bi-temporal provenance and NO injected trading signal. That single constraint plus existential entity-resolution (삼성전자=Samsung Electronics=005930 must collapse to one node or the graph is noise) resolves most verdicts before any cost question: schema-guided/ontology-constrained extraction ENFORCED via function-calling/JSON-schema is the rigorous, low-hallucination, auditable choice; open IE is poison for node collapse (non-canonical predicates) and is the one to avoid as the primary KG builder. Faithfulness is not one technique but a stack: mandatory span+timestamp grounding, cheap self-consistency/confidence, and NLI-entailment verification reserved for high-stakes edges. Output schema itself is a deliverable: {canonical_id (DART corp_code / SEC CIK / ticker), relation, target, source_span, event_time, ingest_time, source_credibility}. Cost tiering exploits the heavy-ingest/1-2-consumer asymmetry: a cheap-to-expensive cascade (hash/MinHash + BGE-M3 near-dup dedup → salience/relevance triage → Haiku-class candidate extraction → Opus-4.8 schema-constrained extraction with span grounding ONLY on high-salience/high-credibility items → NLI only on high-stakes edges), and triage must NOT be language-blind because cheap models are weaker in Korean. Citations are inline (source + arXiv id) since the schema has no citations field.

evaluations.name: Open Information Extraction (LLM free-form triples)
evaluations.whatItIs: Prompt an LLM to emit (subject, predicate, object) triples with no fixed schema; predicates are surface strings drawn from the text. Surveyed as the high-recall but error-prone end of KG construction (LLM-empowered KG construction survey, arXiv:2510.20345; DIAL-KG, arXiv:2603.20059).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Bad fit for KR+EN at this rigor bar. Because predicates and entity strings are non-canonical, '삼성전자가 HBM을 공급' and 'Samsung supplies HBM' produce different nodes AND different relation labels — exactly the node-collapse failure the task flags as 'graph is noise.' Cheap to run (fits heavy ingest) but pushes the entire reconciliation burden downstream onto entity-resolution + relation-canonicalization, which is harder than just constraining extraction up front. Errors in the open stage 'persist and are magnified in fusion' (survey, arXiv:2510.20345).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Predicate explosion (hundreds of near-synonym relations); cross-lingual duplication of nodes/edges; no schema to validate against, so faithfulness can only be checked post-hoc. Acceptable ONLY as a recall-boosting candidate-generation pass feeding a canonicalization step, never as the graph of record.
evaluations.cost: Lowest per-item LLM cost, but high hidden cost: downstream dedup/canonicalization compute + human distrust of a noisy graph. Net-negative at this rigor bar.

evaluations.name: Schema-guided / ontology-constrained extraction
evaluations.whatItIs: Extraction is constrained to a predefined financial ontology (entity types: Company, Person, Product, Event, Catalyst; relation types: supplies, competes_with, owns, guides_to, sentiment_about). The LLM fills a typed template rather than inventing predicates (TEXT2KGBENCH-style conformance; LLM-empowered KG survey, arXiv:2510.20345).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Best fit for the human-judges + KR+US constraints. A fixed relation vocabulary is the only thing that makes KR and EN extractions land on the SAME edge type, so a bilingual graph actually collapses. The ontology is where the no-signal GOAL is enforced structurally: sentiment and catalysts are first-class node/edge ATTRIBUTES carrying source attribution and a provenance weight ('종토방 post X expressed bullish sentiment on 005930 at T'), never aggregated by the system into a buy/sell. Slower/pricier per item than open IE but the rigor is worth it given time-is-not-a-constraint. Use a hybrid/evolving schema (TRACE-KG arXiv:2604.03496; DIAL-KG arXiv:2603.20059) so genuinely new relation types can be proposed for human review rather than silently dropped.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Schema gaps → missed relations (recall ceiling); ontology maintenance is ongoing human work; an over-rigid schema discards novel supply-chain links. Mitigate with an 'unknown_relation' escape hatch queued for human/ontology review.
evaluations.cost: Frontier-model tokens per high-value item; one-time + ongoing ontology curation. The expensive tier of the cascade — applied selectively, not to every crawled item.

evaluations.name: Structured output (JSON-schema / function calling, strict mode)
evaluations.whatItIs: The transport/enforcement mechanism: constrain the model's output to a JSON schema (Claude output_config.format / strict:true tool use) so the extraction is guaranteed well-formed and parseable, with enum-constrained type and relation fields.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Not a competitor to schema-guided extraction — its enforcement layer. The recommended design is 'schema-guided extraction emitted through strict function calling.' Directly serves rigor: enum-constrained relation fields make illegal/hallucinated predicates structurally impossible, and required source_span / event_time / ingest_time fields force provenance at generation time rather than hoping for it. For 1-2 consumers the serving side is trivial; this matters on the INGEST side, where malformed extractions would otherwise corrupt the DB. Note Claude limitation: structured outputs are incompatible with the citations feature, so span-grounding is done as explicit schema fields, not via the citations API.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Schema-valid is not the same as faithful — the model can emit a well-formed but unsupported triple, so this must be paired with span grounding + verification. Very large/recursive schemas degrade; keep per-call schema scoped to one document's entities.
evaluations.cost: Negligible marginal cost over an unconstrained call (one-time schema-compile, then 24h cache). Pure upside; always on for the extraction tier.

evaluations.name: Span-based NER + Relation Extraction
evaluations.whatItIs: Two-stage: detect entity mentions as character spans (NER), then classify relations between span pairs (RE). Every node/edge is tied to exact source offsets by construction.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Strong fit for the provenance mandate — spans ARE the grounding, giving the cleanest audit trail for a human ('anchor-constrained framework with provenance tracking', MDPI Computers 15(3):178). High precision and cheap to run at scale on the heavy-ingest stream. Weaker on the open, qualitative, cross-document relations the user wants ('broad, qualitative organization'): classic RE struggles with implicit/long-range relations and needs per-language models for KR vs EN. Best used as a precision/grounding backbone that an LLM schema-pass enriches, not as the sole extractor.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Recall drops on relations not seen in training and on cross-sentence/cross-doc links; needs separate Korean and English NER/RE models (cross-lingual transfer is imperfect); brittle on noisy community text (종토방/Reddit slang). Caveat: pair with the LLM schema pass to cover open relations.
evaluations.cost: Cheap per item (small specialized models, runnable locally) — excellent for the always-on pre-extraction grounding pass; the cost is model maintenance per language.

evaluations.name: Financial ontology (typed schema) vs schema-free emergent graph
evaluations.whatItIs: Design choice: commit to a curated financial ontology (companies, people, products, events, supply-chain, sentiment, catalysts) versus letting node/edge types emerge from the data with no fixed schema.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The rigor bar + bilingual + human-trust constraints decide this firmly for the ontology. Schema-free emergent graphs maximize flexibility but 'compromise logical consistency' and make cross-lingual node collapse and downstream verification far harder (LLM-empowered KG survey, arXiv:2510.20345); the human can't trust a graph whose edge semantics drift per document. The ontology is also the natural home for the no-signal guarantee: catalysts/sentiment are typed, source-attributed, provenance-weighted attributes — observations, never the system's recommendation. Adopt a DYNAMIC ontology (DIAL-KG arXiv:2603.20059; TRACE-KG arXiv:2604.03496): fixed core for consistency, with a human-gated path to admit new types so the schema isn't a straitjacket.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Pure schema-free → situational/avoid here (inconsistency, verification-hard). Pure static ontology → recall ceiling + maintenance burden. The dynamic-hybrid mitigates both but needs a human review queue, which suits a 1-2-person team.
evaluations.cost: Human curation time (the binding cost, not compute) — acceptable since time is not a constraint and consumers are few.

evaluations.name: Provenance grounding: source span + bi-temporal stamp on every node/edge
evaluations.whatItIs: Every extracted node/edge stores the exact source span, the source document/credibility, event_time (when the fact occurred/was stated) AND ingest_time (when crawled). Quadruplet/bi-temporal modeling (head, relation, tail, time) per temporal-KG practice (ScienceDirect S0893608024000595).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Mandatory, non-negotiable for this design — it is the spine, not an option. It satisfies both the provenance mandate ('lack of provenance makes it fundamentally impossible to verify faithfulness', anchor-constrained framework, MDPI 15(3):178) and the temporal/point-in-time constraint: a human drawing conclusions must filter to what was KNOWN at event_time to avoid lookahead bias, which only bi-temporal stamping (event-time vs ingest-time) supports. It also enables source-credibility weighting since each edge knows its origin (종토방 vs DART).
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Storage overhead and schema discipline; event_time is sometimes ambiguous in community text (must default conservatively to ingest_time and flag low-confidence). If skipped, the whole rigor/anti-lookahead premise collapses — so there is no 'cheaper without it' option.
evaluations.cost: Storage + schema enforcement only; on a single-node graph+vector store at 1-2-consumer scale this is trivial. Cheapest high-leverage component.

evaluations.name: Confidence scoring + self-consistency
evaluations.whatItIs: Attach a confidence to each extraction; optionally sample the extractor multiple times and keep only triples that recur (self-consistency), feeding a per-edge confidence the human can sort/filter on.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Good fit and cheap relative to verification. Gives the human a rankable trust signal that combines with source credibility (so a high-confidence extraction from a 종토방 rumor is still down-weighted by provenance, addressing the 'LOUD over TRUE' risk where pure centrality ranks pump noise above truth). Self-consistency is selectively applied — only where a single pass is uncertain — to control cost on the heavy stream.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated alone; must be fused with source credibility and (for high stakes) NLI, not trusted raw. Self-consistency multiplies cost linearly with samples — gate it on uncertainty, don't run it on everything.
evaluations.cost: Confidence field: ~free. Self-consistency: N× extraction cost on the subset it runs on — keep N small (3) and the subset narrow.

evaluations.name: NLI / entailment verification of extracted edges
evaluations.whatItIs: Run a natural-language-inference check that the source span ENTAILS the extracted triple; reject or flag edges below an entailment threshold (NLI-based faithfulness; GraphEval arXiv:2407.10793; HalluGraph arXiv:2512.01659; traceable LLM validation, ScienceDirect S0306457325000706).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: High-value but must be RESERVED, not universal. It is the strongest defense against the plausible-but-unsupported triple — the exact failure that erodes human trust — and pairs naturally with the span grounding (the span is the NLI premise). But running NLI on every edge of a heavy continuous ingest is wasteful; apply it only to high-stakes edges (e.g., supply-chain links, catalysts, anything from low-credibility sources that nonetheless ranks high). A small DeBERTa/RoBERTa NLI model runs locally and cheaply on that subset.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: NLI models are themselves imperfect and weaker in Korean than English (must use a multilingual/Korean NLI model or translate-then-check); thresholds need tuning; full-coverage NLI is cost-prohibitive. Caveat: scope to high-stakes/high-impact edges only.
evaluations.cost: Local small-model inference on a filtered subset — cheap per checked edge, but cost scales with how much you check, hence the gating.

evaluations.name: Cheap pre-filter tier: hash/MinHash + BGE-M3 embedding near-dup + salience/relevance triage
evaluations.whatItIs: Before any expensive extraction: exact-hash and MinHash drop literal/near-literal reposts; BGE-M3 dense embeddings cluster semantic near-duplicates (the same rumor reworded across 종토방/Reddit); a small salience/relevance classifier drops off-topic chatter. (BGE-M3 tops MTEB-Korean retrieval, BAAI; Secure Multifaceted-RAG arXiv:2504.13425.)
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Exactly the lever the scale-asymmetry constraint calls for — heavy ingest, tiny serving, so spend compute filtering, not LLM-ing everything. Embedding-dedup is essential for adversarial community sources where the same pump is copy-pasted thousands of times; collapsing those before extraction both cuts cost AND prevents fake 'consensus' from inflating importance. BGE-M3 is the right embedder because it is strong in BOTH Korean and English (one model for the bilingual stream) with an 8K context for long DART/EDGAR filings.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Over-aggressive dedup can merge genuinely distinct events that happen to be phrased alike; salience classifier needs Korean-competent training data or it silently drops KR signal. Tune dedup thresholds per source; calibrate salience per language.
evaluations.cost: Embedding + hashing is cents-per-million-items vs dollars for frontier extraction — this tier is what makes the whole pipeline affordable. Runs locally.

evaluations.name: Model-tier triage: small-model candidate extraction → frontier-model schema extraction
evaluations.whatItIs: Two LLM tiers: a cheap Haiku-class model does candidate entity/relation flagging and routing on survivors of the pre-filter; the expensive Opus-4.8 schema-constrained pass runs only on high-salience/high-credibility items, with span grounding attached.
evaluations.fitForThisCase: Directly answers 'can't LLM every crawled item.' Triage must NOT be language-blind: cheap models are materially weaker in Korean, so KR items need either a stronger triage tier or a Korean-tuned small model, else KR signal is lost at the cheap stage. Local vs API at this scale: pre-filter + NER/RE + NLI run locally (privacy on community data, near-zero marginal cost); the frontier schema pass uses the Claude API (Opus 4.8, $5/$25 per 1M tok) on the filtered minority — batch API halves that for non-latency-sensitive backfill, and prompt caching the ontology/system prompt cuts repeat input cost ~90%.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: A cheap-triage false-negative permanently drops an item before the good model sees it — most dangerous for Korean; mitigate with a language-aware threshold and periodic frontier-model audits of dropped KR items. Tier boundaries need monitoring as source mix shifts.
evaluations.cost: Haiku $1/$5, Sonnet $3/$15, Opus 4.8 $5/$25 per 1M tok (current). Batch API = 50% off for backfill; prompt caching the fixed ontology prefix slashes repeated input cost. Cost concentrates on the small high-value fraction that reaches Opus.

evaluations.name: Bilingual KR+EN / cross-lingual extraction and entity linking
evaluations.whatItIs: Handling Korean and English (and code-switched) text end-to-end, and — the existential part — linking mentions across languages to ONE canonical node via canonical registries (DART corp_code, KIND, SEC CIK, ticker 005930). Entity resolution = record-linkage + cross-lingual disambiguation + canonicalization to a persistent ID (Entity-Resolved KGs as the foundation for GraphRAG, ODSC; cross-lingual temporal entity alignment, ScienceDirect S0893608024000595).
evaluations.fitForThisCase: The make-or-break constraint. Cross-lingual entity LINKING is NOT something the extraction prompt solves alone — it must resolve to external authoritative registries so 삼성전자 / Samsung Electronics / 005930 all carry the same canonical_id. The extraction layer's job is to emit the mention + span; a dedicated linker maps it to the registry key (deterministic match on ticker/CIK/corp_code first, embedding+alias fallback for fuzzy names). Frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.8/Sonnet 4.6) are genuinely strong bilingually for the extraction itself; BGE-M3 covers cross-lingual alias similarity for the fuzzy linking step.
evaluations.assumptionsOrFailureModes: Ambiguous names (parent vs subsidiary, ticker reuse, romanization variants), entities absent from registries (private suppliers, foreign names in KR text), and time-varying identity (ticker changes, M&A) — handle the last with bi-temporal identity. Without registry-anchored linking the bilingual graph fragments into noise; this is the single highest-risk component.
evaluations.cost: Registry ingestion/refresh (DART/KIND/EDGAR are free/public); linking is embedding similarity + deterministic key lookup — cheap, local. The cost is engineering the resolver and curating an alias table, not compute.

landscape.recommendation:
Build the extraction layer as a provenance-first, schema-guided pipeline whose output IS the deliverable schema — every node/edge = {canonical_id (DART corp_code | SEC CIK | ticker), relation (enum), target, source_span, source_id+credibility, event_time, ingest_time, confidence} — emitted through strict function-calling so illegal predicates and missing provenance are structurally impossible. Use a fixed-core, human-extensible financial ontology (companies/people/products/events/supply-chain), with sentiment and catalysts as source-attributed, provenance-weighted ATTRIBUTES, never aggregated into a buy/sell — that is how the no-injected-signal GOAL is enforced in the data model, not by policy. Reject open IE as the graph of record (non-canonical predicates break KR+EN node collapse); allow it only as an optional recall-boosting candidate pass feeding canonicalization.

Concrete cost-tiering cascade (cheap→expensive, exploiting heavy-ingest / 1-2-consumer / single-node asymmetry — rule OUT Kafka/Spark/distributed-Neo4j; this is batch+incremental on one box):
1. Exact-hash + MinHash dedup — drop literal reposts. (~free, local)
2. BGE-M3 embedding near-dup clustering — collapse the same rumor reworded across 종토방/Reddit/X BEFORE extraction, so fake consensus can't inflate importance; one model covers KR+EN, 8K ctx for DART/EDGAR filings. (cents/M, local)
3. Salience/relevance triage — small classifier drops off-topic chatter; MUST be Korean-competent or it silently discards KR signal. (cheap, local)
4. Cheap-model candidate extraction (Haiku-class, $1/$5) — flag entities/relations, route. Language-aware threshold so weak-Korean triage doesn't false-negative KR items off the cliff.
5. Frontier schema-constrained extraction (Opus 4.8, $5/$25) — runs ONLY on high-salience/high-credibility survivors; strict JSON schema + span grounding + bi-temporal stamps. Prompt-cache the ontology/system prefix (~90% input savings); Batch API (50% off) for non-urgent backfill.
6. Verification, selectively: self-consistency only where a single pass is uncertain (small N); local NLI/entailment (DeBERTa-class, multilingual) ONLY on high-stakes edges (supply-chain, catalysts, high-rank-but-low-credibility) — the source_span is the NLI premise.
7. Cross-lingual entity LINKING as a dedicated step (not the prompt): deterministic match to canonical registry (ticker/CIK/corp_code) first, BGE-M3 alias-similarity fallback, bi-temporal identity for ticker changes/M&A. This is the existential component — if nodes don't collapse, nothing else matters.

Importance ranking must fold provenance/credibility, not pure topology — weight each edge by source credibility × confidence so TRUE outranks LOUD; centrality alone ranks pump noise first. Run pre-filter, NER/RE grounding, NLI, and linking LOCALLY (privacy on community data, near-zero marginal cost); reserve the Claude API for the small high-value fraction. Layered stack: typed graph (structure) + vectors (BGE-M3 semantic retrieval) GraphRAG-style, with Obsidian as optional human presentation. (Sources cited inline above: LLM-empowered KG survey arXiv:2510.20345; DIAL-KG arXiv:2603.20059; TRACE-KG arXiv:2604.03496; anchor-constrained provenance MDPI Computers 15(3):178; GraphEval arXiv:2407.10793; HalluGraph arXiv:2512.01659; traceable LLM validation ScienceDirect S0306457325000706; cross-lingual temporal entity alignment ScienceDirect S0893608024000595; Entity-Resolved KGs for GraphRAG, ODSC; BGE-M3 / MTEB-Korean, BAAI & arXiv:2504.13425; Claude pricing per claude-api skill, 2026-06.)
risks: Entity resolution is the single point of failure: if cross-lingual linking to canonical registries (삼성전자=Samsung=005930) is weak, the graph fragments into duplicate nodes and every downstream rigor measure is wasted. Mitigate with registry-anchored deterministic matching + bi-temporal identity; audit collapse rate continuously.

Language-blind cost tiering silently loses Korean signal: cheap triage/extraction models are weaker in Korean, so KR items can be false-negatived off the cascade before the good model sees them. Requires Korean-aware thresholds and periodic frontier-model audits of dropped KR items.

Schema-valid is not faithful: strict function calling guarantees well-formed output, not truthful output. The model can emit a clean but unsupported triple. Span grounding + selective NLI are mandatory companions, not optional — without them the rigor is cosmetic.

Importance ranking can rank LOUD over TRUE: adversarial sources (종토방/Reddit/Telegram pump) copy-paste consensus. If importance uses pure graph centrality, fake consensus dominates. Provenance/credibility weighting on edges and pre-extraction embedding dedup are required, not nice-to-have.

Bi-temporal discipline is fragile in community text: event_time is often ambiguous in 종토방/Reddit posts; defaulting wrong reintroduces lookahead bias for the human. Default conservatively to ingest_time, flag low-confidence event_time, and never let the human filter on an unverified event_time.

Ontology rigidity vs drift: a static ontology caps recall on novel supply-chain/catalyst relations; a too-loose one reintroduces the inconsistency that breaks node collapse. The dynamic-hybrid (fixed core + human-gated new types) needs an actually-maintained review queue, or it degrades to one extreme.

Verification cost can balloon if NLI/self-consistency are applied broadly instead of gated to high-stakes edges — undermining the whole cost-asymmetry rationale. Gating discipline must be enforced and monitored as source mix shifts.

methods.summary: This layer turns the bilingual KR+EN knowledge graph plus its attached time series into a navigable, RANKED, CLUSTERED, HIERARCHICALLY-SUMMARIZED structure for a human — and stops short of any buy/sell call. The rigor is won in four places, not in picking a fancy centrality: (1) entity resolution must collapse 삼성전자=Samsung Electronics=005930 BEFORE any graph metric, anchored on hard market identifiers (ticker/ISIN/CIK/DART corp_code) so the graph isn't noise; (2) every fact carries bi-temporal stamps (valid/event-time + transaction/ingest-time) so a human reading a 'conclusion' as-of date T never sees information that arrived after T (no look-ahead); (3) importance must fold SOURCE CREDIBILITY into edge and teleport weights (TrustRank/personalized-PageRank seeded on DART/SEC/tier-1 press) so the ranking surfaces TRUE over LOUD — raw PageRank/eigenvector centrality on adversarial 종토방/Reddit/Telegram data ranks pump-volume, which is a design failure; (4) info-flow (does information arrival lead price/another series?) is the part most likely to produce confident garbage, so it is gated by stationarity (ADF/KPSS on returns not prices), conditional/multivariate transfer entropy (not bivariate — bivariate TE invents common-driver edges), IAAFT surrogate-data significance tests, and Benjamini-Hochberg/Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR correction across the thousands of pairs tested. Crucially this layer reports correlation/lead-lag/info-flow as ANNOTATED, SIGNIFICANCE-TESTED, EFFECT-SIZED EDGES with explicit caveats — never as a recommendation. Clustering uses Leiden (not Louvain — Louvain can yield internally-disconnected communities) for the graph-topology hierarchy, and BERTopic→HDBSCAN over multilingual embeddings for the text/theme hierarchy, fused, then GraphRAG-style map-reduce LLM summarization rolls leaf communities up into nested theme summaries that yield the ranked keywords + clustered conclusions the user wants. Scale is correctly read as heavy-ingest / 1-2 consumers, so everything is single-node graph+vector with batch+incremental recompute; Kafka/Spark/distributed-Neo4j are ruled out. LLM cost is tiered: dedup → embedding-similarity near-dup collapse → salience/credibility pre-filter run cheaply on everything; expensive LLM extraction and community summarization run only on what survives.

methods.method: Entity resolution / cross-lingual record linkage (deterministic identifier anchoring + multilingual-embedding blocking + LLM adjudication on hard pairs) — PRE-REQUISITE to every graph metric
methods.measures: identity (which surface forms = which canonical entity); collapses 삼성전자/Samsung Electronics/005930 into one node
methods.inputType: text mentions + structured identifiers (ticker, ISIN, SEC CIK, DART corp_code, KIND code) + entity embeddings
methods.assumptions: a high-precision identifier exists for the entity class that matters most (listed equities — it does: ticker/ISIN/CIK/corp_code); multilingual encoder maps KR and EN surface forms near each other; transliteration/aliases are enumerable for the long tail
methods.failureModes: silent under-merge (삼성전자 ≠ Samsung Electronics → graph fragments, centrality meaningless) or over-merge (Samsung Electronics merged with Samsung SDI/parent chaebol → false edges); ticker reuse/recycling across delisted firms; homographs (사과=apple/apology); person vs company name collisions
methods.rigorNotes: This is the single highest-leverage rigor step — every downstream centrality, cluster, and causality number is invalid if nodes don't collapse. Use a three-tier cascade: (1) deterministic join on identifiers = ground truth, never LLM-overridden; (2) embedding blocking (multilingual SBERT, e.g. paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2) to generate candidate pairs cheaply; (3) LLM adjudicates ONLY ambiguous candidate pairs (cost-tiered). Maintain a versioned alias/cross-lingual dictionary; measure precision/recall against a hand-labeled KR+EN gold set; track merge decisions bi-temporally so a wrong merge can be unwound as-of a date without rewriting history
methods.cost: Identifier join: trivial. Embedding blocking: cheap (amortized, one encode per mention). LLM adjudication: bounded — only borderline pairs, the dominant cost lever. Re-resolution is incremental per new mention.

methods.method: Bi-temporal modeling (valid/event-time + transaction/ingest-time) on every node, edge, and claim
methods.measures: point-in-time correctness: what was knowable as-of date T vs what is known now; enables as-of-T graph snapshots
methods.inputType: every fact/edge/claim with two timestamps: when it became true in the world (event/valid time) and when the system recorded it (ingest/transaction time)
methods.assumptions: source documents expose or imply an event time (filing date, post timestamp, article publish time); late-arriving and back-dated data are expected and must be representable
methods.failureModes: look-ahead bias (a human draws a conclusion 'as of 2024-03' using a fact that only arrived 2024-06) — the cardinal sin for a human investor; survivorship bias (delisted entities dropped from the graph); revision/correction overwrites that destroy the prior state
methods.rigorNotes: Non-negotiable for defensibility. Two independent time axes per fact (bitemporal, per Snodgrass/SQL:2011 and XTDB-style stores). All retrieval and all centrality/causality computations are parameterized by an as-of transaction-time AND valid-time window, so any ranked keyword or clustered conclusion is reproducible exactly as it would have read at time T. Corrections append new versions; nothing is destructively updated. This also makes the info-flow analysis honest: information-arrival series are built on event-time, not ingest-time.
methods.cost: Storage overhead (every fact versioned) — acceptable at this scale. Query cost: temporal filter on indexed time columns, cheap on single node. Main cost is discipline in the ingest pipeline, not compute.

methods.method: Weighted / Personalized PageRank with credibility-folded edge weights and TrustRank-style teleport (the importance backbone)
methods.measures: global importance/authority of entities and claims, with provenance folded in so importance ≠ loudness
methods.inputType: directed weighted graph; edge weight = f(co-occurrence strength, recency, AND source credibility); teleport vector seeded on trusted nodes
methods.assumptions: link/co-occurrence structure carries signal; a defensible credibility prior exists per source (DART/SEC/exchange filings = high; tier-1 press = high; 종토방/Reddit/X/Telegram = low and adversarial); graph is reasonably connected after entity resolution
methods.failureModes: WITHOUT credibility folding: ranks pump-and-dump volume — a coordinated 종토방/Telegram campaign manufactures co-mentions and PageRank rewards the loudest, not the truest (the explicit failure the user warned about); topic-drift; dangling nodes; PageRank on a near-disconnected graph concentrates mass artificially
methods.rigorNotes: Fold credibility in TWO places: (a) edge weights down-weight edges originating from low-credibility sources; (b) personalized teleport (TrustRank, Gyöngyi et al. 2004) jumps preferentially to a trusted seed set (regulators/filings), so trust propagates outward and adversarial subgraphs can't bootstrap their own authority. Report importance as a provenance-decomposed score (how much of this entity's rank comes from trusted vs adversarial edges) so a human sees WHY something ranks. Damping factor 0.85 standard; verify power-iteration convergence; recompute incrementally.
methods.cost: Sparse power iteration, single node, seconds-to-minutes even at 10^5–10^6 nodes. Cheap. Recompute on a batch cadence + incremental on graph deltas.

methods.method: Eigenvector centrality, HITS (hub/authority), betweenness, k-core decomposition (complementary structural lenses, NOT primary ranking)
methods.measures: eigenvector: influence weighted by neighbors' influence; HITS: hubs (curators/aggregators) vs authorities (primary sources); betweenness: brokers/bridges between clusters (contagion paths); k-core: dense cohesive cores vs periphery
methods.inputType: the same weighted directed graph (HITS on directed; eigenvector/k-core typically on weighted undirected projection)
methods.assumptions: each captures a DIFFERENT notion of importance; none is 'the' answer; betweenness assumes shortest-path information flow
methods.failureModes: eigenvector centrality localizes/blows up on high-degree hubs and is even more easily gamed by manufactured co-mention than PageRank (no teleport escape) — do NOT use as primary on adversarial data; betweenness is O(VE), expensive, and unstable to single edge changes; raw HITS suffers topic-drift / mutual-reinforcement spam
methods.rigorNotes: Use as ANNOTATIONS on the credibility-weighted PageRank backbone, not as the ranking. HITS authority on a credibility-weighted graph is a useful cross-check that primary sources (high authority) outrank aggregators (high hub). k-core is excellent for separating a dense genuinely-discussed core from low-k rumor periphery — a cheap, hard-to-game cohesion filter. Always report which centrality, on which (credibility-weighted) graph, as-of which time; never blend into one opaque number.
methods.cost: Eigenvector/HITS: cheap (power iteration). k-core: near-linear, cheap. Betweenness: expensive O(VE) — approximate (Brandes sampling) or restrict to top-subgraph; run on a slower cadence.

methods.method: Leiden community detection, applied recursively for a nested hierarchy (graph-topology theme structure)
methods.measures: clustering / community structure on the graph; a mutually-exclusive collectively-exhaustive partition at each of several hierarchy levels (C0 root → leaf)
methods.assumptions: modular community structure exists; quality function (modularity/CPM) reflects meaningful grouping; resolution parameter chosen/swept sensibly
methods.failureModes: Louvain (the predecessor) can produce internally DISCONNECTED communities — a correctness bug, the specific reason to choose Leiden; modularity resolution limit hides small communities; resolution choice changes the answer; unstable across runs without fixed seed
methods.rigorNotes: Choose Leiden over Louvain specifically because Leiden guarantees well-connected communities and is the GraphRAG standard. Run recursively to build the nested hierarchy (each community re-partitioned into sub-communities to leaf level), giving the multi-level theme tree the user wants. Fix the random seed for reproducibility; sweep resolution and report stability (e.g., consensus over seeds / variation of information) rather than a single fragile partition. Communities are computed on the CREDIBILITY-WEIGHTED graph so adversarial co-mention clusters are damped.
methods.cost: Near-linear, fast on single node even at large scale. Recompute batch + incremental.

methods.method: BERTopic (multilingual embeddings → UMAP → HDBSCAN → c-TF-IDF) for text/theme clustering, with HDBSCAN hierarchy (semantic theme structure, fused with the graph communities)
methods.measures: clustering of TEXT into topics/themes with human-readable keyword signatures; density-based hierarchy of themes; native outlier/noise handling
methods.inputType: document/post/claim embeddings (multilingual SBERT spanning KR+EN); raw text for c-TF-IDF keywords
methods.assumptions: semantically similar texts are near in embedding space across languages; density structure exists; UMAP+HDBSCAN params (n_neighbors, min_cluster_size, min_samples) tuned
methods.failureModes: UMAP is stochastic → non-reproducible topics unless random_state fixed; HDBSCAN dumps low-density items into a noise/-1 bucket (can be large); over-/under-clustering driven by min_cluster_size; multilingual encoder may split a KR cluster from its EN equivalent if the encoder is weak — cross-lingual topic fragmentation
methods.rigorNotes: This is the SEMANTIC complement to Leiden's STRUCTURAL communities — fuse them (graph community ∩/∪ topic) rather than trusting either alone; agreement between an embedding-density cluster and a graph community is strong evidence the theme is real, not an artifact. Fix UMAP random_state for reproducibility (documented BERTopic best practice). Use a strong multilingual encoder (paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2 or better) so KR+EN co-cluster. Treat the HDBSCAN noise bucket explicitly — it is often where genuine novelty AND genuine junk both live; route it to the credibility filter, not silently into a theme. Optionally LLM-refine topic labels (cost-tiered).
methods.cost: Embedding: cheap, amortized, reused from the vector layer. UMAP+HDBSCAN: moderate, batch-recomputed; incremental approximations exist. c-TF-IDF: cheap. LLM label refinement: optional, bounded.

methods.method: Mutual information (nonlinear association screen between information-arrival series and price/other series)
methods.measures: correlation (nonlinear, undirected statistical dependence) — a screening filter before causal/directional tests
methods.inputType: two (discretized or KSG-estimated continuous) time series: e.g., credibility-weighted mention/sentiment arrival rate vs returns/volume
methods.assumptions: sufficient samples for density/entropy estimation; appropriate binning or k-NN (KSG) estimator; series aligned on EVENT time
methods.failureModes: estimator bias with small samples and high dimensionality; sensitive to binning; detects dependence but says nothing about direction or causation; non-stationarity inflates apparent MI (spurious)
methods.rigorNotes: Use as a CHEAP nonlinear screen to decide which pairs are even worth the expensive directional tests — not as evidence of anything causal. Compute on STATIONARY transforms (returns, differenced/normalized arrival rates), not raw prices/levels. Significance via permutation/shuffle surrogates. Always paired with FDR correction because you screen many pairs. Report as 'association', explicitly NOT direction or cause.
methods.cost: Cheap per pair; the screen that keeps the expensive transfer-entropy stage affordable across many pairs.
